content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "game_name": "Archero",
  "genre": "Casual",
  "core_mechanics": "The game operates from a top-down perspective. Player movement is controlled directly by input, stopping automatically when no movement input is detected. When the player is stationary, they automatically attack the nearest enemy with projectiles. While moving, the player ceases attacking to focus on dodging. \n\nGameplay progresses through 'rooms' within 'chapters' (referred to as 'levels' in this specification). To clear a room, the player must defeat all enemies within it. Upon clearing a room or leveling up (which occurs after a certain number of enemies are defeated, separate from chapter levels), the player is presented with a choice of one out of three random skills. These skills (e.g., 'Front Arrow +1' for an additional projectile, 'Ricochet' for bouncing projectiles, 'Attack Speed Up') stack and persist for the duration of the current 'run'. \n\nPlayer and enemies possess health points (HP). Projectiles deal damage upon collision. When the player's HP drops to zero, the current run ends, and the player is returned to the main menu. Gold and permanent talent upgrades persist across runs. Gold is collected from defeated enemies and room clears. \n\nState tracking includes: player's (x, y) coordinates, current HP, attack stats (damage, speed, projectile count), active skills, current gold, and accumulated score. For enemies, their (x, y) coordinates, HP, type, and attack patterns are tracked. Projectiles track their (x, y) coordinates, velocity, and damage. Game flow tracks the current room, current level, enemies remaining in the room, and overall game state.",
  "visual_elements": "All visual elements will be rendered using p5.js shapes and basic image assets.\n\n**Player:** A green upward-pointing triangle or simple archer sprite (e.g., 20x20 pixels). Displays a small health bar above or below.\n**Enemies:**\n    *   **Melee:** Red square (e.g., 25x25 pixels).\n    *   **Ranged:** Red circle (e.g., 20px radius).\n    *   **Bosses:** Larger, distinct red polygon or complex shape (e.g., 50x50 pixels), with a prominent health bar.\n**Projectiles:**\n    *   **Player:** Small blue circles (e.g., 5px radius).\n    *   **Enemy:** Small red circles (e.g., 5px radius).\n**Rooms:**\n    *   **Walls:** Thick grey or brown rectangles (e.g., 10px thickness).\n    *   **Floor:** Dark grey or brown background color.\n    *   **Doors:** Rectangular gaps in walls, visually indicated as open (no barrier) or closed (barrier visible).\n**UI Elements:**\n    *   **Score Display:** Text in the top-right corner.\n    *   **HP Bar:** Horizontal bar for player (bottom-left) and smaller bars above enemies.\n    *   **Level/Room Indicator:** Text in the top-left corner.\n    *   **Skill Choice Overlay:** A translucent background covering most of the screen, displaying three rectangular buttons with skill names/icons.\n    *   **Gold Icon:** Small yellow circle or coin sprite.\n    *   **Permanent Upgrade Menu:** A menu overlay with buttons and text for various upgrades.\n\n**Animations/Effects:**\n    *   **Player Attack:** Brief flash or ripple effect on the player when firing a projectile.\n    *   **Enemy Hit:** Brief color tint (e.g., red) on the enemy sprite and HP bar update.\n    *   **Enemy Death:** Enemy sprite disappears, potentially with a small burst particle effect.\n    *   **Projectile Impact:** Small spark or puff effect at the collision point.\n    *   **Room Clear:** 'ROOM CLEARED!' text briefly displayed, followed by doors opening and skill choice overlay appearing.\n    *   **Level Transition:** 'CHAPTER X - [Level Name]' text fading in and out centrally.",
  "control_scheme": "Keyboard controls will be mapped as follows:\n\n*   **Movement:**\n    *   `W` or `ArrowUp`: Move player upwards.\n    *   `A` or `ArrowLeft`: Move player left.\n    *   `S` or `ArrowDown`: Move player downwards.\n    *   `D` or `ArrowRight`: Move player right.\n    *   (Note: Player automatically stops attacking when any movement key is pressed and resumes attacking when all movement keys are released).\n\n*   **Skill Selection (when Skill Choice Overlay is active):**\n    *   `Space`: Select the first displayed skill option.\n    *   `Z`: Select the second displayed skill option.\n    *   `Shift`: Select the third displayed skill option.\n\n*   **Menu Navigation (Main Menu, Permanent Upgrades, Game Over):**\n    *   `ArrowUp`/`ArrowDown`: Navigate between menu options.\n    *   `Space`: Select the highlighted menu option.\n\n*   **Pause:**\n    *   `Escape`: Toggle pause menu.",
  "game_states": "The game will transition through several distinct states:\n\n1.  **Main Menu:** Initial state upon loading the game. Displays 'Start Game', 'Permanent Upgrades', and 'High Scores' options.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Start Game' selected: -> **Loading Chapter 1** (brief display) -> **Playing**.\n        *   'Permanent Upgrades' selected: -> **Permanent Upgrades Menu**.\n        *   'High Scores' selected: -> **High Scores Display**.\n\n2.  **Permanent Upgrades Menu:** Allows players to spend accumulated gold on permanent character enhancements. Displays available upgrades, current gold, and 'Back' option.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Back' selected: -> **Main Menu**.\n\n3.  **High Scores Display:** Shows the player's best scores.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Back' selected: -> **Main Menu**.\n\n4.  **Playing:** The core gameplay loop. Player moves, attacks, and dodges in a room.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   All enemies in room defeated: -> **Skill Selection** (if skill points available or room clear) or directly to **Loading Next Room**.\n        *   Player HP reaches 0: -> **Game Over**.\n        *   `Escape` pressed: -> **Paused**.\n\n5.  **Skill Selection:** An overlay appears, presenting three random skills for the player to choose from. Game pauses during this state.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   Player selects a skill: -> **Loading Next Room** (if not already the last room) or **Loading Next Level** (if room was last in level) -> **Playing**.\n\n6.  **Loading Next Room/Level:** A brief intermediary state displaying 'Loading...' or 'CHAPTER X - [Level Name]' before rendering the next room/level.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   Loading complete: -> **Playing**.\n\n7.  **Paused:** Game is frozen, and a pause menu is displayed ('Resume', 'Main Menu').\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Resume' selected or `Escape` pressed: -> **Playing**.\n        *   'Main Menu' selected: -> **Main Menu**.\n\n8.  **Game Over:** Player's run has ended. Displays final score, 'Game Over' message, and options ('Retry', 'Main Menu').\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Retry' selected: -> **Loading Chapter 1** -> **Playing** (starts a new run).\n        *   'Main Menu' selected: -> **Main Menu**.\n\n9.  **Win Condition:** Player has cleared all rooms in the final level (Level 5). Displays 'VICTORY!', final score, and options ('Main Menu').\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   'Main Menu' selected: -> **Main Menu**.",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins by successfully clearing all rooms in the final level (Level 5), culminating in the defeat of the final boss in the last room of that level. A 'VICTORY!' screen will be displayed, along with the final score.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses if their character's health points (HP) reach 0 at any point during a run. This will trigger the 'Game Over' state.",
  "scoring_system": "The game incorporates a detailed scoring system to track player performance:\n\n*   **Points for Enemy Kills:**\n    *   Small/Basic Enemy: +10 points\n    *   Medium/Elite Enemy: +25 points\n    *   Large/Special Enemy: +50 points\n    *   Mini-Boss: +100 points\n    *   Final Boss: +500 points\n\n*   **Points for Room Clearance:**\n    *   Base +50 points per room clear.\n    *   Additional +10 points for each enemy killed within that room (e.g., clear a room with 5 enemies = 50 + (5*10) = 100 points).\n\n*   **Points for Level Completion:**\n    *   Base +100 points per level completed.\n    *   Additional +25 points for each room within that level (e.g., complete Level 1 with 5 rooms = 100 + (5*25) = 225 points).\n\n*   **Bonus Points:** There are no specific bonus multipliers for speed or combo in this simplified version. All points are additive.\n\n*   **High Score System:** The game will track the highest score achieved across all runs using `localStorage`. This high score will be accessible from the main menu.\n\n*   **Score Display:** The current run's score will be prominently displayed in the **top-right corner** of the game screen, formatted as 'SCORE: [current_score]'.\n\n*   **Gameplay Effect:** The score system does not directly affect gameplay (e.g., no power-ups based on score). It serves purely as a metric for player performance and achievement.",
  "level_system": "The game features 5 progressively challenging levels (chapters):\n\n**1. Level 1: The Training Grounds**\n    *   **Rooms:** 5 rooms.\n    *   **Difficulty:** Easy.\n    *   **Progression:** Introduces basic movement and auto-attack. Enemies are slow-moving, simple melee types (3-5 per room). No ranged enemies. Basic enemy HP and damage. Room layouts are simple, mostly open, with minimal obstacles.\n    *   **Transition:** 'CHAPTER 1 - The Training Grounds' display.\n\n**2. Level 2: The Shifting Sands**\n    *   **Rooms:** 7 rooms.\n    *   **Difficulty:** Moderate.\n    *   **Progression:** Introduces ranged enemies (1-2 per room, alongside 3-4 melee). Enemies move slightly faster and have increased HP. Room layouts become more complex, incorporating more walls and some indestructible blocks, requiring more tactical movement.\n    *   **Transition:** 'CHAPTER 2 - The Shifting Sands' display.\n\n**3. Level 3: The Dark Forest**\n    *   **Rooms:** 10 rooms, including one dedicated mini-boss room (e.g., Room 7).\n    *   **Difficulty:** Challenging.\n    *   **Progression:** Enemy count increases (4-5 faster melee, 2-3 faster ranged). New enemy types with basic patrol patterns or slower, stronger projectiles are introduced. Enemy HP and damage are significantly higher. Room layouts are more intricate, with dynamic elements like temporary barriers or falling debris, forcing constant movement. The mini-boss has moderate HP and a simple, predictable attack pattern.\n    *   **Transition:** 'CHAPTER 3 - The Dark Forest' display.\n\n**4. Level 4: The Crystal Caves**\n    *   **Rooms:** 12 rooms, featuring two mini-boss rooms.\n    *   **Difficulty:** Hard.\n    *   **Progression:** High density of enemies (6-8 total per room), including faster, more aggressive melee and ranged types. Introduces new enemy mechanics, such as enemies that explode on death or split into smaller foes. Player projectiles might be slower or more numerous to balance the increased challenge. Room layouts are mazelike, with shifting walls or environmental hazards (e.g., damaging floor tiles).\n    *   **Transition:** 'CHAPTER 4 - The Crystal Caves' display.\n\n**5. Level 5: The Ancient Fortress**\n    *   **Rooms:** 15 rooms, culminating in a final boss room.\n    *   **Difficulty:** Very Hard.\n    *   **Progression:** The ultimate challenge. Features the highest enemy density and variety, including 'elite' versions of previous enemies with enhanced stats or unique abilities. Room layouts are highly complex and dynamic, often requiring precise dodging. The final boss battle is multi-phased, with intricate attack patterns and high HP, demanding mastery of all learned skills and mechanics.\n    *   **Transition:** 'CHAPTER 5 - The Ancient Fortress' display.\n\n**Advancement:** Players advance to the next room by defeating all enemies in the current room. Upon clearing the last room of a level, the player progresses to the next level.\n\n**Transition Display:** When transitioning between levels, a central text display will appear for 3 seconds, showing 'CHAPTER [X] - [Level Name]'.\n\n**Level Indicator:** The current level and room will be displayed in the **top-left corner** of the screen, formatted as 'LEVEL: [current_level] / ROOM: [current_room_in_level]'.",
  "simplifications": "To streamline implementation for an HTML5 environment with p5.js and Matter.js, the following simplifications are made:\n\n*   **Multiplayer Modes:** The 'Hero Duel' (1v1 PvP) and any other multiplayer aspects are entirely removed. The game will be a single-player experience.\n*   **Procedural Generation:** While the original mentions procedurally generated worlds, for this implementation, room layouts will be derived from a set of predefined templates for each level. These templates can be randomly selected and rotated to simulate variety rather than truly generative algorithms. Enemy spawn points within these templates will be randomized based on room type and difficulty.\n*   **Equipment System:** The complex 'equipment' system (drops, stats, inventory) is simplified. Players will primarily collect 'Gold' from defeated enemies and room clears. This gold is then used in a dedicated 'Permanent Upgrades' menu to enhance character stats (HP, damage, attack speed) rather than managing specific gear slots or item rarities.\n*   **Talent Tree:** The 'permanent talent' upgrades are abstracted into a simpler system. Instead of a complex branching talent tree, it will be a more linear or simplified selection of upgrades available for gold, focusing on core stats like Max HP, Attack Damage, Attack Speed, and Gold Gain.\n*   **Graphics & Art Style:** The game will rely on p5.js's native drawing capabilities for shapes, lines, and basic colors. Simple sprites or placeholder images will represent characters and objects, foregoing complex 3D models, advanced particle systems, or detailed high-fidelity assets.\n*   **Physics Engine:** Matter.js will be used specifically for robust collision detection between game objects (player, enemies, projectiles, walls). Movement will be kinematic (direct position manipulation based on input/AI logic) rather than physics-driven for player and enemies, focusing on responsive control over realistic physics.",
  "implementation_notes": "P5.js will be the primary framework for game rendering, input handling, and overall game state management. Matter.js will be integrated for efficient and accurate collision detection between the player, enemies, projectiles, and room walls. The game loop will be managed by p5.js's `draw()` function.\n\n*   **Score Display:** A dedicated p5.js text element will render the current score at the specified top-right screen position, updated live.\n*   **Level Progression:** The game strictly adheres to the 'no endless games' and 'at least 3-5 levels' requirement. Each level has a defined number of rooms, and completion of all rooms in a level triggers a transition to the next, as detailed in the `level_system`.\n*   **Permanent Progression:** Gold collected persists between runs and can be spent on permanent upgrades from the Main Menu. These upgrades will modify base player stats that are applied at the start of every new run.\n*   **Run-based Skill System:** Skills chosen during a run (e.g., 'Front Arrow +1') are temporary and reset upon death, enforcing the roguelite 'run' structure.\n*   **Enemy AI:** Enemies will have simple AI patterns: melee enemies pursue the player, ranged enemies maintain distance and fire projectiles. Bosses will have scripted attack patterns.\n*   **Collision Handling:** Matter.js will define rigid bodies for player, enemies, projectiles, and walls. Collision events will trigger damage calculations, projectile destruction, and room clear checks."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>
      <p id="gameControls" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_controls}</p>
    </div>
    <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/p5.js/1.4.0/p5.js"></script>
    <script src="https://unpkg.com/p5.collide2d@0.7.3/p5.collide2d.js"></script>
    <script type="module" src="game.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

Welcome to Archero! Battle through 5 challenging chapters filled with dangerous enemies. When standing still, you automatically attack the nearest foe. Move to dodge incoming attacks, but you'll stop shooting while moving. Clear all enemies in each room to progress. After each room clear, choose one of three powerful skills to enhance your abilities. Collect gold to purchase permanent upgrades between runs. Can you conquer all 5 chapters and defeat the final boss? Archero

Welcome to Archero! Battle through 5 challenging chapters filled with dangerous enemies. When standing still, you automatically attack the nearest foe. Move to dodge incoming attacks, but you'll stop shooting while moving. Clear all enemies in each room to progress. After each room clear, choose one of three powerful skills to enhance your abilities. Collect gold to purchase permanent upgrades between runs. Can you conquer all 5 chapters and defeat the final boss?

Arrow Keys / WASD: Move your character (Up, Left, Down, Right)
Space: Select first skill option during skill selection
Z: Select second skill option during skill selection
Shift: Select third skill option during skill selection
Enter: Start game from main menu
Escape: Pause/Unpause game
R: Return to main menu from game over screen
